coredata.dc:title: Novel biocatalytic systems for maintaining the nucleotide balance based on adenylate kinase immobilized on carbon nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Hetmann, Anna
Wujak, Magdalena
Bolibok, Paulina
Zięba, Wojciech
Wiśniewski, Marek
Roszek, Katarzyna
coredata.dc:description: In this study graphene oxide (GO), carbon quantum dots (CQD) and carbon nanoonions (CNO) have been characterized and applied for the first time as a matrix for recombinant adenylate kinase (AK, EC 2.7.4.3) immobilization. AK is an enzyme fulfilling a key role in metabolic processes. This phosphotransferase catalyzes the interconversion of adenine nucleotides (ATP, ADP and AMP) and thereby participates in nucleotide homeostasis, monitors a cellular energy charge as well as acts as a component of purinergic signaling system. The AK activity in all obtained biocatalytic systems was higher as compared to the free enzyme. We have found that the immobilization on carbon nanostructures increased both activity and stability of AK. Moreover, the biocatalytic systems consisting of AK immobilized on carbon nanostructures can be easily and efficiently lyophilized without risk of desorption or decrease in the catalytic activity of the investigated enzyme. The positive action of AK-GO biocatalytic system in maintaining the nucleotide balance in in vitro cell culture was proved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adenylate kinase
Graphene oxide
Carbon quantum dots
Carbon nanoonions
Enzyme immobilization
Nucleotide balance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption and desorption of AK on carbon nanostructures
Lyophilization of native and immobilized AK
AK enzymatic activity and stability assay
In vitro activity of AK-GO biocatalytic systems
Geobacillus stearothermophilus AK ovesrproduction in E. coli BL21-CodonPlus(DE3)-RIL cells
Adsorption and desorption of AK on carbon nanostructures
In vitro activity of AK-GO biocatalytic systems

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of tested carbon nanomaterials and photographs of their aqueous solutions (GO - a, b, c; CQD - d, e, f; and CNO - g, h, i).
UV–vis spectra for tested carbon nanomaterials (GO, CQD and CNO).
FTIR (upper) and RAMAN (bottom) spectra of tested carbon materials. The shadowed area is non-analyzable due to the diamond absorption.
FTIR spectra of native AK and biocatalytic systems obtained after immobilization of AK on CNO, CQD and GO.
Viability of CHO cells exposed to GO, CQD and CNO in time- and concentration-dependent manner.

SDS-PAGE of Geobacillus stearothermophilus adenylate kinase obtained after two-step purification procedure. Lanes: 1-bacterial lysate, 2-lysate after denaturation at 60 °C, M-molecular mass marker, 3-lysate after denaturation at 70 °C, 4-sample after Blue-Sepharose affinity chromatography. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The activity of biocatalytic systems and native adenylate kinase in the direction of ATP formation.
The activity of biocatalytic systems and native adenylate kinase in the direction of ADP formation.
The viability of mesenchymal stem cells exposed to AK, GO and AK-GO biocatalytic system with or without 5 mM ATP as cytotoxic agent.
The protein adsorption capacity of the carbon nanostructures.
AK activity before and after lyophilization.

row.entry: AK activity before lyophilization [μmole ATP/min/mg protein]
AK activity after lyophilization [μmole ATP/min/mg protein]
AK activity after lyophilization (% of initial activity)

head.ce:title: Novel biocatalytic systems for maintaining the nucleotide balance based on adenylate kinase immobilized on carbon nanostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biology and Environmental Protection, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń, 1 Lwowska St., 87-100 Toruń, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Biology and Environmental Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Physicochemistry of Carbon Materials Research Group, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń, 7 Gagarin St., 87-100 Toruń, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Physicochemistry of Carbon Materials Research Group

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: INVEST-TECH R&D Center, 32-34 Płaska St., 87-100 Toruń, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study graphene oxide (GO), carbon quantum dots (CQD) and carbon nanoonions (CNO) have been characterized and applied for the first time as a matrix for recombinant adenylate kinase (AK, EC 2.7.4.3) immobilization. AK is an enzyme fulfilling a key role in metabolic processes. This phosphotransferase catalyzes the interconversion of adenine nucleotides (ATP, ADP and AMP) and thereby participates in nucleotide homeostasis, monitors a cellular energy charge as well as acts as a component of purinergic signaling system. The AK activity in all obtained biocatalytic systems was higher as compared to the free enzyme. We have found that the immobilization on carbon nanostructures increased both activity and stability of AK. Moreover, the biocatalytic systems consisting of AK immobilized on carbon nanostructures can be easily and efficiently lyophilized without risk of desorption or decrease in the catalytic activity of the investigated enzyme. The positive action of AK-GO biocatalytic system in maintaining the nucleotide balance in in vitro cell culture was proved.

ce:section.ce:para: The main difficulty in wide-ranging enzymes application is their instability in the working conditions or during storage. To improve the activity and stability, enzymes have usually been immobilized on a solid support [1–3]. Despite the huge diversity of immobilization matrices and methods, there are still some limitations to be overcome. For example, the adsorption method gives in general less stable biocatalytic systems but allows for enzyme structure maintenance that is in opposite to the covalent conjugation. Nanomaterials in general can serve as excellent supporting materials for enzyme immobilization. Carbon-based nanomaterials are characterized by high mechanical strength, as well as the unique surface properties, which allow more effective adsorption of biological molecules including enzymes [4,5]. Despite the vast research interest in novel nanobiocatalytic systems, the finding of support for stable and resistant enzyme adsorption is still the key challenge.

The other solution to overcome the disadvantages of enzymes low catalytic efficiency or instability is construction of artificial enzymes that mimic both structural and functional aspects of natural enzymes [6–8]. The design of enzyme mimics is a promising area of research with great potential to impact on our daily life. However, there are some challenges that have to be overcome. First of all, there are still gaps in complete understanding of certain reaction pathways or in correct identification of the transition states and intermediates of reactions, which lessens our ability to produce effective enzyme mimics. Moreover, in the case of nano-sized enzyme mimics, we have to reduce their toxicity and enhance their specificity to allow their application in vivo [8].

Adenylate kinase (AK, EC 2.7.4.3) is a phosphotransferase that catalyzes the reversible phosphoryl transfer as follows: MgATP2− + AMP2− ⇔ MgADP− + ADP3− [9–11]. The enzyme can directly control the concentration of all three adenine nucleotides (AMP, ADP and ATP) and participate in nucleotide homeostasis and energetic metabolism [10,12–14]. Adenylate kinases act as sensors with great accuracy for the energy state of cells that can respond to various stressors [14–17]. For example, within the cell AK functions as a regulator of the metabolic sensors activity, cell division and differentiation, whereas in the extracellular space it is a part of purinergic signaling system [14]. Purinergic signaling uses extracellular nucleotides and nucleosides as signaling molecules. Through activation of nucleotide/nucleoside receptors, these molecules influence many short-term (acute) physiological processes, e.g. immune response, inflammation, platelet aggregation and vascular tone as well as long-term (trophic) processes, including cell proliferation, migration, differentiation, and death [18]. The nucleoside and nucleotide homeostasis in the extracellular microenvironment is governed by an extensive network of membranous ecto-enzymes metabolizing nucleotides consisting of hydrolases: ecto-nucleoside triphosphate diphosphohydrolases (ecto-NTPDases), ecto-nucleotide pyrophosphohydrolases/phosphodiesterases (ecto-NPPs), ecto-alkaline phosphatases (ecto-ALP), and ecto-5′-nucleotidase (ecto-5′-NT) as well as phosphotransferases which are capable of ATP resynthesis: ecto-adenylate kinase (ecto-AK) and ecto-nucleoside diphosphate kinase (ecto-NDPK) [11,19–21]. It is worth to note, that some of these enzymes can also be secreted into the extracellular space and therefore contribute to the extracellular nucleotide metabolism [22,23].

Catalytic function of adenylate kinases is particularly important in tissues with high and variable energy requirements [14]. Several AK isoforms have been identified in eukaryotic cells, differing in size, cell location, and kinetic properties in accordance with the metabolic requirements of different cell types [14]. Adenylate kinases integrate metabolism by adjusting the cellular energy charge to the metabolism requirements. Adenylate kinases regulate many intra- and extracellular endoenergetic processes, such as nuclear transport, hormone secretion, ciliary motility, cell cycle energy or DNA repair and synthesis. The important role of these enzymes in cell metabolism is shown by mutations in AK-encoding genes resulting in abnormal cell function [14]. By controlling energy levels and distributing AMP signals, adenylate kinases are also important regulators of intracellular signaling pathways and stress responses [14,24,25].

Adenylate kinases from different species exhibit high sequence and structure homology. The enzymatic protein is composed of a central core domain (CORE), a LID domain of a variable length, AMP binding domain and ATP binding domain with a conserved P-loop motif [14].

The potential use of AK in biological systems requires an increase in its enzymatic activity, stability and resistance, and when it is desirable, even modulation of its catalytic properties. Efficient biodistribution and overcoming of cell barriers are other necessary features of enzymes meant for use in biological systems. It is assumed that achieving these effects is possible due to enzyme immobilization. For this purpose, nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO), carbon quantum dots (CQD), and carbon nanoonions (CNO) were tested for the first time as the immobilization support for recombinant adenylate kinase.

We used the following reagents: KCl, KOH, HClO4, NaCl, EDTA, glycerol, glucose, n‑heptane, DMSO, β‑mercaptoethanol and Tris-base (Avantor Performance Materials, Gliwice, Poland, p.a. grade); KH2PO4, K2HPO4, TBA (tetrabutylammonium hydrogen sulfate) and isocratic methanol of HPLC grade (Baker, Phillipsburg, USA); ampicillin, nucleotides (AMP, ADP, ATP, CTP, GTP, UTP and TTP, 96%–99% purity), MTT (3‑(4,5‑dimethylthiazol‑2‑yl)‑2,5‑diphenyltetrazolium bromide), APS (ammonium persulfate), TEMED (N,N,N′,N‑tetramethylethylenediamine), acrylamide, bisacrylamide (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, Mo., USA); IPTG (isopropyl β‑d‑1‑thiogalactopyranoside) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA); yeast extract and tryptone (Merck, Kenilworth, USA). For SDS-PAGE, molecular weight markers were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. Blue Sepharose was kindly provided by Dr. A. Leźnicki (Department of Biochemistry, NCU in Torun).

GO was synthesized using the modified Hummers method [26]. A mixture of concentrated H2SO4/H3PO4 (360:40 mL) was poured into graphite flakes (3 g). KMnO4 (18 g) was slowly added to the mixture, producing a slightly exothermic conditions (35–40 °C). After 24 h of continuous stirring, 400 mL of water and 5 mL of 30% H2O2 was added producing a bright yellow sol. The mixture was centrifuged at 15000g for 30 min, and the supernatant was removed. The remaining material, in a form of gel, was next washed successively with water and ethanol, and centrifuged repeatedly (several times) to adjust the pH ~5.

In the typical experimental procedure, 2.0 g of gelatin were dissolved in 100 mL water at 50 °C under agitation. Subsequently, the mixture was poured into a stainless steel autoclave with a glass liner of 300 mL capacity and heated at 200 °C and 180 bar. After 6 h process the reactor was cooled down to room temperature. The resulting brownish solution was centrifuged at 15000g for 30 min to remove weight precipitate and agglomerated particles.

High-purity (5 N) graphite rod (diameter of 6 mm) was used as the anode and the cathode. Both electrodes were connected with a digital controllable DC power supply and submerged in deionized water in a Pyrex beaker (3 L). The voltage and current amplitudes of the DC power supply were set to 20 V and 50 A, respectively, monitored on a digital oscilloscope.

The produced carbons were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The images were taken using a transmission electron microscope F20X-TWIN (FEI-Tecnai) operated at 200 kV. The drop of sample solution was placed on a Cu grid coated with an ultrathin amorphous carbon film, and then dried under ambient condition.

UV–vis absorption was carried out using a Jasco 660 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) measurements were accomplished by Bruker Vertex 70 infrared spectrophotometer using Platinum diamond ATR techniques in the frequency range 60–4000 cm−1.

The nonpolarized Raman scattering spectra of carbon structures were investigated in the spectral range of 60–4500 cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded in the backscattering geometry using SENTERRA micro-Raman system. As an excitation light we used the green laser operating at 532 nm. The laser beam was tightly focused on the sample surface through a 50× microscope objective. To prevent any damage of the sample, an excitation power was fixed at 2 mW. The resolution was 4 cm−1, CCD temperature 223 K, laser spot 20 μm, and total integration time 100 s (50 × 2 s), objective 50×. The position of the microscope objective with respect to the sample was piezoelectrically controlled (XY position).

Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Cells were grown in F-12 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C in humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. Twenty four hours before the experiment was started, 5 × 104 cells (in a volume of 25 μL) were seeded to each well of a 12-well plate. GO, CQD and CNO nanostructures were added to the growing cells in concentrations of 1, 10, and 50 μg mL−1, respectively, and incubated for the next 24 h. Subsequently, MTT test based on the ability to reduce 3‑(4,5‑dimethylthiazol‑2‑yl)‑2,5‑diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) by mitochondrial dehydrogenases, was performed in triplicate for assessing cell metabolic activity and viability.

cDNA sequence encoding Geobacillus stearothermophilus AK was synthesized de novo by GeneCopoeia, Inc. (Rockville, USA). The cDNA was cloned into pET-3a(+) vector between the NdeI and BamHI sites. Recombinant plasmid pET-3a(+) was transformed into E. coli BL21-CodonPlus(DE3)-RIL cells using heat shock method. A single transformant colony was used to inoculate 5 mL of LB medium (0.5% yeast extract, 1% tryptone, 1% NaCl, pH 7.0) containing 50 μg mL−1 ampicillin, 34 μg mL−1 chloramphenicol and 0.5% glucose. The culture was incubated overnight at 30 °C with shaking. Then bacteria were transferred to 0.5 L of fresh LB medium supplemented with components as above and grown in the same conditions until the OD600 of 0.6 was reached. The cells were induced to the Geobacillus stearothermophilus AK synthesis by addition of IPTG to the final concentration of 1.0 mM and subsequently cultured with shaking for 3 h at 37 °C and then overnight at 20–22 °C. After that the bacterial culture was cooled on ice and centrifuged at 3000g for 30 min at 4 °C. The cell pellet was resuspended in buffer A (25 mM HEPES-NaOH Tris-HCl, pH 7.6). E. coli cells were sonicated and the bacterial lysate was centrifuged at 20000g for 20 min at 4 °C. The obtained supernatant was used for further procedures.

The first AK purification step was the two-stage heat denaturation. The bacterial supernatant was incubated for 15 min at 60 °C, centrifuged at 20000g for 20 min at 4 °C, incubated again for 15 min at 70 °C and centrifuged as above. The portion of partially purified AK containing about 50 mg of proteins was subjected to affinity chromatography on Blue-Sepharose (1.5 cm × 10 cm) column equilibrated with buffer A. Proteins weakly bound to the resin were removed from the column using buffer A and then AK was eluted with NaCl gradient (0.3 to 0.8 M NaCl in the buffer A) at a velocity of 0.8 mL/min. Fraction with the highest AK activity were pooled and subsequently dialyzed overnight against buffer A and concentrated by centrifugation using Amicon® Ultra Centrifugal filters (Merck, Kenilworth, USA). The presence and purity of the recombinant Geobacillus stearothermophilus AK were verified by SDS-PAGE [27]. Protein samples were incubated for 20 min at 85 °C in reducing buffer containing 4% SDS and 10% β‑mercaptoethanol, centrifuged at 5000g for 3 min at room temperature and subsequently run through 4% stacking gel and 12% separating gel. The protein concentration was determined using Bradford assay [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and desorption of AK on carbon nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The purified Geobacillus stearothermophilus AK was mixed with the buffered solution (50 mM HEPES-NaOH pH 7.6) of graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots and carbon nanoonions in a ratio 1:1 (w/w) and incubated at 4 °C. The AK adsorption stability on carbon nanostructures was tested every 24 h for 9 days. 100 μL of the mixture containing the biocatalytic system was centrifuged at 14000g for 20 min at 4 °C using 50 kDa Millipore Ultra Centrifugal filters (Millipore) to separate the desorbed enzyme. Protein content was analyzed in the obtained filtrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lyophilization of native and immobilized AK
ce:section.ce:para: Homogenous preparation of native AK and AK immobilized in 1:1 ratio on GO, CQD and CNO were freeze-dried by centrifugation under vacuum using CentriVap concentrator with cold trap (Labconco). After evaporating to dryness, the samples were dissolved in 50 mM HEPES-NaOH buffer pH 7.6. Protein content and adenylate kinase activity were analyzed in the obtained lyophilizate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AK enzymatic activity and stability assay
ce:section.ce:para: Adenylate kinase activity assays were carried out in a volume of 50 μL for 15 min at 37 °C. The reaction mixtures containing substrate (1 mM ADP or 1 mM ATP and AMP) and 2 mM MgCl2 in 25 mM HEPES-NaOH buffer, pH 7.6 were used. The reaction was terminated by addition of 50 μL of 1 M HClO4. After 5 min incubation on ice, samples were neutralized with 50 μL of 1 M KOH and delipidated by shaking with n‑heptane (1:5, v/v). The samples were centrifuged as above and purine nucleotide concentrations were analyzed by HPLC. For stability evaluation, the native and immobilized AK were stored for 9 days and their activity was determined as above. The activity of native AK measured at day 0 was taken as a 100% control.

The qualitative and quantitative analysis of purine nucleotides in the reaction mixtures was performed by the HPLC method as described before [29]. Purine nucleotides were separated in isocratic conditions on the Chromolith Performance RP-18e column, 4.6 mm × 100 mm (Merck, Kenilworth, USA). The samples were eluted using 0.1 M phosphate buffer, pH 7.0 containing 25 mM TBA, 5 mM EDTA, and 3% methanol. Purines were detected at λ = 260 nm and identified based on the retention times of standard nucleotide solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro activity of AK-GO biocatalytic systems
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse mesenchymal stem cells (mMSC) from bone marrow were purchased from Gibco. Cells were grown in low-glucose DMEM medium supplemented with 2 mM l‑glutamine and 10% FBS, in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C in humidified atmosphere. Twenty four hours before the experiment was started, 5 × 104 cells (in a volume of 25 μL) were seeded to each well of a 12-well plate. AK, GO and AK-GO biocatalytic systems were added to the growing cells in concentration of 30 μg mL−1, with or without 5 mM ATP, respectively. After 48 h incubation, MTT test was performed in triplicate for assessing cell metabolic activity and viability. The viability of cells growing without any supplementation was taken as a 100% control.

The experiments were conducted at least two times in triplicate and qualitatively identical results were obtained. The data are expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD). The statistical analysis was done using the Student's t-test, with p < 0.05 taken as statistically significant.

Fig. 1 . shows the HRTEM pictures of carbon nanomaterials used for immobilization. We have chosen such set of materials bearing in mind the maximal differences in both, structural and chemical, properties. GO (Fig. 1a–b) forms flexible, flat, hundred-nanometer sheets, while CQD (Fig. 1d–e) and CNO (Fig. 1g–h) are spherical in nature with diameter ca. 10 nm. On the other hand, CNO consist of several graphene layers while the other materials are rather amorphous carbons.

In our previous studies [3], we proved that the modified Hummers method allowed for synthesis of homogenous, large-sized GO sheets as shown in Fig. 1a–b. GO large size sheets form numerous folds created due to the strong interaction of the surface functionalities. These functionalities cause that instead of the large-size-sheet, after dispersion in water GO forms the brownish and stable solution, meaning that each layer exists separately (Fig. 1c). CQD and CNO also form stable water solutions, most probably, due to the small size of the individual nanoparticles (Fig. 1f, i).

Presented in Fig. 2 the UV–vis results confirm the differences in chemical nature of tested carbons. GO spectrum possesses two signals at 230 and ca. 315 (shoulder) nm originating from oxygen functionalities. In the CNO spectrum the broad and mutually overlapped bands with the maxima at 246 and 266 nm are observed. These bands appear as a result of the presence of polyaromatic structures on the CNO surface. The absorption spectrum of the CQD sample displays three characteristic peaks at 245, 275 and 315 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bond. The presence of a absorption peak exhibited at around 310 nm, can be attributed to the n–π* transition of CO bond.

The strong differences between surface chemistry are also revealed by IR and Raman data (Fig. 3 ). In the IR spectrum of GO, the bands of surface OH, CO, and CO functionalities are present (Fig. 3a). The spectrum is characterized by identifying its absorption bands appearing due to adsorbed water in OH stretching region at 3600–2700 cm−1 and OH bending at 1620 cm−1. Moreover, the bands of CO stretching at 1733 cm−1 and CO stretching at 1074 cm−1 are also present. Following the literature data (e.g. [30]), one can assign the peak at 1360 cm−1 to the OH deformation and bending vibration of interlayer water. The covering of the graphene sheet with the oxygen groups is extremely high, i.e. 28% m/m, from elemental analysis, causing that high transparency of the sample is expected.

In the IR spectrum of CQD, the red-shifting –OH stretching vibrations up to 3100–2900 cm−1 together with the presence of the strong IR bands at 1560 (νasym) and 1385 (νsym) cm−1, means that carboxylic surface groups form hydrogen bonds. The presence of amine functionalities is here proved by the bands at 1650 cm−1 (shoulder) of NH2-bending and 1275 cm−1 from CN stretching as well as NH wagging at 760 cm−1. The surface CO structures are confirmed by the band at 1100 cm−1. Both samples are characterized with similar Raman spectra (Fig. 3b) which display two prominent peaks corresponding to the well-documented G and D bands. The G band is usually assigned to the E2g phonon of C sp2 atoms, while the D band is associated with structural defects and disorders that can break the symmetry and selection rules [31,32].

High amount of heteroatom (O,N) on the surface of GO and CQD should cause their high surface polarity and in consequence formation of hydrogen bonds with proteins. Contrary to the above, the CNO sample is completely opaque in whole tested IR-range. This means, in the case of carbonaceous materials, that graphitic structures are dominating in the sample. This statement/finding has been confirmed in Raman analysis. In the spectrum there is strong G-band at 1574 cm−1 and IG/ID ratio as high as 11.1. The 2D signal at 2695 cm−1, its high symmetry and low half width mean that the CNO material is in fact a several-layer rolled graphene without surface functional groups. In this case, contrary to the above, one can expect mainly π-π interactions with immobilized proteins.

The FTIR spectra of native AK and the enzyme immobilized on carbon nanomaterials are presented in Fig. 4 . The spectrum of native AK is represented by four main groups of signals, i.e. amide I–III as well as δ(CH) at 1460 cm−1. The changes in the amide I band due to immobilization are subtle and not recognized without mathematical treatment as deconvolution and/or curve fitting. Contrary, immobilization causes broadening as well as decreasing in the intensity of the amide II band for all three supports.

Cytotoxicity of GO, CQD, CNO was tested in vitro on Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells. Both GO (Fig. 5a) and CQD (Fig. 5b) have no cytotoxic effects on the CHO cells and are fairly biocompatible in the tested concentration range from 1 to 100 μg mL−1. In turn, CNOs exhibit cytotoxicity at concentrations above 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. 5c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geobacillus stearothermophilus AK ovesrproduction in E. coli BL21-CodonPlus(DE3)-RIL cells
ce:section.ce:para: Using the bacterial expression system based on the strain BL21-CodonPlus (DE3)-RIL and the pET-3a(+) expression vector we have successfully accomplished the synthesis of Geobacillus stearothermophilus AK. AK present in the bacterial supernatant fraction was purified to the electrophoretic homogeneity using two step purification procedure employing partial purification by thermal denaturation and affinity chromatography on Blue Sepharose. The obtained adenylate kinase is approximately 23 kDa in size which is in accordance with a molecular weight of AK from Geobacillus stearothermophilus. Thermal treatment combined with Blue-Sepharose chromatography is a relatively fast, easy and efficient method for obtaining a homogenous AK preparation (Fig. 6 ) necessary for further immobilization studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and desorption of AK on carbon nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption efficiency of the enzyme adsorbed onto the surface of carbon nanostructures was investigated by determining the protein content in a filtrate obtained after centrifugation of the immobilized enzyme preparations using 50 kDa cut-off filters. The most effective adsorption is observed for graphene oxide, but the immobilization on carbon quantum dots and carbon nanoonions was also very efficient (Table 1 ). In the case of GO, the yield over 90% of the starting amount of AK protein used for immobilization was achieved. Adsorption of AK on the surface of CQD effected in approximately 64% of protein adsorbed and immobilization of AK on the surface of CNO has only slightly lower efficiency as compared to CQD. It is worth highlighting, that we do not observe any rapid decrease in AK adsorption onto all analyzed nanomaterials during the whole 9 days storage time. The obtained results clearly indicate that the tested biocatalytic systems are stable.

Figs. 7 and 8
summarize the activity of biocatalytic systems in comparison to the native adenylate kinase. The adsorption of adenylate kinase on GO, CQD and CNO results in a clear increase in both enzyme activities. The activity of AK in the direction of ATP synthesis (Fig. 7) increases approx. 2.5 times as compared to the native enzyme and the increase is higher than in the direction of ADP formation (Fig. 8).

In the control sample containing a high-purity native adenylate kinase stored at 4 °C for 9 days, the AK activity in both directions maintains at a constant level. It indicates that the highly purified protein is characterized by a high enzymatic stability over time. The increase in AK activity after adsorption on nanostructures also persists during 9 days incubation. The high and stable activity of biocatalytic systems remains also after lyophilization – Table 2 , that makes them of great usefulness for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro activity of AK-GO biocatalytic systems
ce:section.ce:para: Summary of the above results led us to the selection of the most beneficial AK-GO biocatalytic system and an attempt to confirm its functionality and usefulness for in vitro applications. In mMSC cell culture we have confirmed the functionality of obtained biocatalytic system, as its supplementation caused the substantial increase in cell viability, especially when exposed to high (5 mM), cytotoxic ATP concentrations (Fig. 9 ). The cell viability rising over 12 times when compared with the cells treated with 5 mM ATP can be explained by the possibility of restoring nucleotide balance and removing the excess of extracellular ATP by AK-GO biocatalytic system.

This study aimed at comparing the physico-chemical and cytotoxic properties of selected carbon nanomaterials as well as their immobilization capability for adenylate kinase (AK) which has promising biomedical potential. AKs catalyze the transfer of the terminal γ-phosphate residue from nucleoside triphosphates (NTP), predominantly ATP, to adenosine monophosphate (AMP) as well as the transfer of the β‑phosphate residue between ADP molecules [9–11]. The main function of AK is maintaining the adenine nucleotide homeostasis within the cell and in the extracellular environment [10–14]. Changes in nucleotide concentrations play important roles in the etiology of many diseases. The formation of thrombosis in the coronary arteries of the heart is initiated by the activation of platelets. ADP is a molecule needed to initiate their aggregation and maintain the repair process of damaged tissue [33–35]. Apart from a significant contribution of AK to metabolism of circulating ADP in the bloodstream [36], the enzyme is also essential for cardiac differentiation, adaptation to stress signals and post-ischemic coronary flow recovery [10,14,25,37].

An uncontrolled increase in ADP concentration amplifies platelet aggregation and thrombus formation. On the other hand, ATP released from damaged cells, initiates inflammatory processes and plaque formation [33,34]. Selective and targeted activation of enzymes specifically degrading ATP and/or ADP appears to be a universal way to regulate blood levels of these nucleotides. The activity of AK leads also to the formation of AMP which can be further converted into adenosine, well known to be a cardioprotective molecule [33,34]. Therefore, it should be considered that regulation of AK activity may become an important component in the treatment of many diseases which are associated with disturbed extracellular nucleotide balance.

The most effective known AK inhibitor is Ap5A [38,39], but activators of these enzymes have been not described so far. Therefore, it is extremely attractive from the medical point of view to immobilize AK on non-toxic (biocompatible) carbon nanostructures which results in a significant and stable increase in catalytic activity of the enzyme.

Beneficial effects of immobilization on carbon nanostructures on the catalytic properties of immobilized enzymes have initiated the process of searching for new and tailored immobilization supports, characterized by appropriate structure, chemical composition and biocompatibility. A particular attention is paid to understand mechanisms for protein immobilization and modulatory effects of supports on enzyme activity and catalytic efficiency [2,3,40,41]. Here, we have synthesized and characterized three different nanostructural carbonaceous materials, among which GO proved to be the most promising as immobilization matrix for biomedical applications. From the presented results one can conclude that this capability effects from GO physicochemical properties together with excellent biocompatibility.

Due to the large area and numerous functional groups, graphene oxide was previously considered as a material suitable for enzyme immobilization. A single layer of graphene oxide is enriched in oxygen-containing groups, which makes it possible to use GO as a vehicle for immobilization without modifying its surface and using additional factors to bind the protein to the carrier [40]. Graphene oxide has been used so far as a substrate for immobilization of enzymes destined for various biotechnological application purposes, such as biosensors, carriers for drugs and nucleic acids, or industrial biocatalysis [42,43].

Zhang et al. [40] have used GO as a matrix for immobilization of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and lysozyme. Both enzymes undergo spontaneous immobilization on GO, but with a various yield. 1 mg of graphene oxide binds up to 100 μg of HRP, while up to 700 μg of lysozyme adsorbs on the same amount of material. Our results indicate that over 900 μg of adenylate kinase can be adsorbed on 1 mg GO. The observed differences in the amount of immobilized protein may indicate that enzyme-matrix interactions depend on the specific charges present on the surface of both the enzyme and the support. In addition, kinetics studies on horseradish peroxidase immobilized on GO indicate that the immobilization reduces its enzymatic activity [40].

In turn, Pavlidis et al. [44] immobilized lipases and bacterial esterases, such as lipase B from Candida arctica or esterase from Bacillus subtilis, on carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide. The authors concluded that the influence of carbon nanomaterials on catalytic activity of the enzymes and immobilization efficiency is largely dependent on the protein used, which means that every biocatalytic system requires individual analyses.

The results presented here clearly show that the applied carbon nanostructures can be a potential support for AK immobilization. However, they differ in structure, surface functionalities, adsorption capacity (GO > CQD = CNO) and biocompatibility (GO > CQD > CNO). The CNO samples in opposite to other two carbon materials are much more graphitized (see Fig. 3). Its high toxicity stays in line with the literature data showing that highly graphitized carbons are much more toxic than amorphous ones [45].

Different structural and chemical nature of the supports induces distinct interactions with proteins. The decreasing in the intensity of amide II caused by immobilization was observed previously for catalase immobilized on GO [3] and means that H-bonding are of decisive importance in the protein interaction with the functionalized surface of carbon nanomaterial. It was expected for GO and CQD samples, however it is astonishing in the case of CNO. Further study to arbitrate the origins of experimentally observed H-bonding is needed. In the literature, there are well described examples of non-classical NH⋯π interactions of amino acids in both, neutral and cationic forms [46,47]. On the other hand, highly graphitized, spherical carbon nanostructures as nanodiamonds or nanoonions are unstable in the neat form and tend to reorganization of the external layer and self-covering with oxygen adatoms [48] which would be responsible for more classic NH⋯O interactions. It needs further study to distinguish which are key interactions. Nevertheless, in all cases the enzyme adsorption is stable and, what is more important, one can observe the substantial and stable increase in the enzymatic activity of AK. The high and stable activity of biocatalytic systems remains also after their lyophilization.

Bioactive molecules often are freeze-dried to stabilize them for storage or distribution. Freeze-drying method is widely accepted as the preferred one for long-term storage of biomolecules and macromolecular assemblies in medical applications. Dried samples can be stored at room temperature and dissolved if needed, which has clear advantages not only for banking and transport but also for in vitro or in vivo analyses [49].

From the biomedical application point of view, we have chosen the AK-GO biocatalytic system and proved its functionality in cell culture. However, it is possible to speculate that the choice of immobilized substrate will depend on the intended purpose of the enzyme. The substantial increase in mesenchymal stem cells viability, especially when exposed to high (5 mM), cytotoxic ATP concentrations, confirmed the in vitro activity of obtained biocatalytic system. ATP is regarded as the most potent activator of pathological processes among all ecto-nucleotides. Secreted during cell stress, ATP acts like DAMP molecule (Danger-Associated Molecular Pattern-molecule) and activates the cell response to stress conditions. As high as micromolar ATP concentrations usually decrease cell proliferation and induce either apoptosis or necrosis in the majority of cells [50,51]. The removal of excess ATP and restoring the nucleotide balance by AK-GO is particularly promising for its prospective applications in biological systems.

The presented studies clearly demonstrate that GO and CQD fulfill most of the conditions required for enzyme immobilization support, i.e. bind the protein with high yield, do not decrease catalytic activity, even after storage or lyophilization. It is extremely important that these materials are non-toxic for living cells. However, in the case of enzymes as AK, expected to act in extracellular environment, GO seems to be the most desirable support due to its size preventing the cellular internalization of AK-GO system. On the other hand, CQD seem to be more suitable when small size supports and intracellular delivery of enzymes or biologically active molecules is required. The preliminary in vitro evaluation confirmed the positive effect of AK-GO biocatalytic system on the cultured cells, especially when exposed to high, cytotoxic ATP concentrations.
